fact: July 13 is exactly 194 days into the year, which means there are only 171 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is peak summer heat season - the sun stays up for over 15 hours in many places, making it perfect for epic water balloon fights!
Cicadas are typically screaming their loudest around this time of year, creating a buzzing symphony that can reach 120 decibels - louder than a chainsaw!
Fireflies often blink most actively in mid-July during their biggest mating season, flashing secret love codes to find their perfect match!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, July 13 falls in the middle of winter, when penguins are huddling together for warmth.
The Cancer zodiac sign rules until July 22, which means people born on July 13 are supposedly extra moody and love to stay home in their shells like crabs.
Sunflowers in the Northern Hemisphere are typically reaching their awkward teenage phase right now - they're tall and gangly but haven't bloomed their giant yellow faces yet.
July 13 sits almost exactly one month after the summer solstice, when Earth starts its slow tilt back toward shorter days (but you won't notice for weeks!).
Mosquitoes are usually at their most annoying peak around this date because hot, humid July weather creates perfect breeding conditions in puddles and ponds.
In northern regions like Alaska, the sun barely sets on July 13 - it just skims the horizon like a giant orange ball playing peek-a-boo.
Thunder and lightning storms often happen most frequently in mid-July because the super-heated air creates perfect conditions for dramatic sky explosions.
Corn plants in farm fields are typically growing so fast around July 13 that you can literally hear them creaking and popping as they stretch toward the sky!
